summary: Ellen F. Eglin (died c. 1915), a Washington, D.C. housekeeper and later Census Office clerk, invented a clothes wringer in the 1880s. In 1888 she sold the rights for $18 rather than patent in her own name, telling The Woman Inventor she feared white customers would refuse a wringer known to come from a Black woman.

claims.object: a clothes wringer whose commercial rights she sold to an agent in 1888 for $18, explaining to Charlotte Smith of The Woman Inventor that she feared market rejection if buyers knew a Black woman had patented it
a charwoman in the U.S. Census Office under the Department of the Interior by 1890, while living with her brother Charles at 1929 11th Street NW in Washington, D.C.
claims.citationLabel: Wikipedia: Ellen Eglin (census register and city directory citations)

historicalContext: Eglin's sale of her wringer is a documented case of how racial barriers shaped who could claim credit and profit from late-19th-century domestic inventions.

summary: Marjorie Stewart Joyner (1896–1994) died in Chicago's Bronzeville after a career as national adviser to Madam C.J. Walker's beauty schools. She patented a permanent-wave machine in 1928 and later helped write Illinois cosmetology law and co-founded national Black beautician associations with Mary McLeod Bethune.

claims.object: U.S. Patent 1,693,515 (November 27, 1928) for a permanent-wave machine that used rods on a table apparatus with a scalp protector so curls or straight styles lasted several days
Madam C.J. Walker's company as national adviser overseeing roughly 200 beauty schools and training thousands of stylists before and alongside her patented wave-machine work

historicalContext: Joyner's machine entered Black and white salon practice even though Walker's company held the patent credit; her later organizing with Bethune built professional infrastructure for African American beauticians.

summary: Christine Darden (b. 1942) joined NASA Langley in 1967 as a human computer, earned a doctorate in engineering, and led sonic-boom research for high-speed flight. She became the first African American woman at Langley promoted into the Senior Executive Service and received the Congressional Gold Medal in 2019.
locationLabel: NASA Langley Research Center, Hampton, Virginia

claims.object: NASA Langley's sonic boom group within the High Speed Research program, designing wing and nose configurations to reduce sonic-boom noise for proposed high-speed civil transport
claims.citationLabel: NASA Langley Hall of Honor: Christine M. Darden

claims.object: the Senior Executive Service as the first African American woman at NASA Langley to reach that federal civil-service rank, later directing strategic communication and education before retiring in 2007
claims.citationLabel: NASA: From Computers to Leaders — Women at NASA Langley

historicalContext: Darden's path from the Langley computer pool to SES leadership continues the West Area Computing lineage associated with Katherine Johnson, Dorothy Vaughan, and Mary Jackson, and she is one of the researchers profiled in Margot Lee Shetterly's Hidden Figures.

summary: Carter G. Woodson (1875–1950) died in 1950 after founding the Association for the Study of Negro Life and History and launching Negro History Week in 1926 — the seed of Black History Month. From his Washington office-home he edited the Journal of Negro History under Jim Crow.

claims.object: the Association for the Study of Negro Life and History (now ASALH) and the Journal of Negro History, anchoring professional Black historical scholarship during the Jim Crow era
claims.citationLabel: NPS: Carter G. Woodson Home National Historic Site

claims.object: Negro History Week in February 1926, later expanded into Black History Month, choosing February to honor the birth months of Abraham Lincoln and Frederick Douglass
claims.citationLabel: NPS: Carter G. Woodson Home — Why February?

historicalContext: Woodson's 'office-home' on 9th Street NW is preserved as a National Historic Site interpreting how he built the early Black history movement from a working scholar's residence.

displayName: Carter G. Woodson Home National Historic Site
summary: The Carter G. Woodson Home National Historic Site protects the Washington, D.C. row house where Dr. Woodson lived and worked while building ASALH and Negro History Week. The National Park Service interprets the office-home as the institutional birthplace of organized African American historical study.

claims.object: Carter G. Woodson Home National Historic Site, a National Park Service unit interpreting Woodson's residence and the early Black history movement
claims.citationLabel: NPS: Carter G. Woodson Home National Historic Site

claims.object: 1538 9th Street NW in Washington, D.C., the office-home from which Woodson edited scholarship and organized ASALH programming
claims.citationLabel: NPS: Carter G. Woodson Home — Basic Information

historicalContext: Acquired for the National Park System in 2005, the home has undergone renovation periods; the park's interpretive program continues online and through partner programming while physical access follows NPS updates.

displayName: Boston African American National Historic Site
summary: Boston African American National Historic Site preserves and interprets Beacon Hill's 19th-century free Black community, the Black Heritage Trail, Underground Railroad networks, and the Massachusetts 54th Infantry Regiment story. Ranger-led and self-guided tours connect meeting houses, residences, and schools that anchored abolitionist Boston.

claims.object: the Black Heritage Trail® and Beacon Hill sites documenting Boston's free Black community, Underground Railroad activity, and Civil War-era service including the Massachusetts 54th Infantry Regiment
claims.citationLabel: NPS: Boston African American National Historic Site

claims.object: the Museum of African American History campus on Joy Street in Beacon Hill, the primary visitor orientation point for the national historic site partnership
claims.citationLabel: NPS: Boston African American National Historic Site — Black Heritage Trail

historicalContext: Beacon Hill's Black community built churches, schools, and mutual-aid institutions that made Boston a northern hub of abolition and enlistment for the 54th Massachusetts.
